data.title: ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF: Still Not For Investors
data.description:
My last article noted that ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF (SCO) was really not for the ordinary investor. The major problem with a leveraged fund (or really leveraged anything) is that you had better be right or the losses really mount up quickly. In this case the Iranian crisis combined with mixed messages from the current United States administration is causing a whole lot of activity involving oil commodity prices. Lots of gyrations are not uncommon due to the mixed messaging. That is not the time to get into a fund that aims to short oil.

Returns

As the fund fact sheet shows, this is definitely not a buy and hold idea.

Note that the fund has done rather well recently (no thanks to the Iranian crisis). Oil prices over the last year have been weak. But to get that benefit, an investor would have had to know when the Iranian issues would begin and more importantly when (and how) that beginning would affect the crude oil markets upon which this ETF is based. Because the Iranian crisis is exactly the opposite of what a fund like this needs.

The other thing to note is that the price of oil has been in a downtrend for three years since 2022. Yet the longer-term returns shown above are negative.

Leverage

This fund is leveraged to produce a 200% return in the opposite direction of oil prices as a general rule.

"This ProShares ETF seeks daily investment results that correspond, before fees and expenses, to -2x the daily performance of its underlying benchmark (the "Daily Target"). While the Fund has a daily investment objective, you may hold Fund shares for longer than one day if you believe it is consistent with your goals and risk tolerance. For any holdlng period other than a day, your return may be higher or lower than the Dally Target."

As the fact sheet notes, the daily investment objective means that the fund itself has got to reset each day to achieve that objective. At the end of this article is the SEC information sheet that goes into even more details on this than I can here.

The problem with leverage is that big changes can happen overnight that are essentially not controllable because you cannot buy or sell. Therefore, holding this after hours given that it moves twice as much as the market can be more excitement than anyone needs.

The expense ratio is 0.95%. That is hardly a consideration for something like this. Actually, the fund itself is administered in a fairly straightforward fashion where the fund is reset to produce another exact duplication of the day before.

This Is Really For Professionals

It might be that professional traders that are very good with charts can use the current situation to make money. But that is very different from the average person.

Furthermore, these things have a tendency to liquidate just before the time comes to make serious money with them. Then the (basically) same type of fund again goes public to start the process all over. Really the entity making money in this situation is the fund advisor.

ETF Weakness

While a fund like this has an accurate daily return. The sum of several daily returns often provides a drift away from the expected result. Some of that can be seen in that the long-term return of the fund is negative despite the fact that the oil price trend is down since 2022.

The reason that this happens is (for example) say the fund reported a 10% loss on the first day. It resets but your principal is now 90%. So if the fund then reports another 10% loss, this time your principal loss is 9% to 81% of the principal left. It is not the same 10% as the day before. So, while the fund reports a 20% loss in two days, your return is negative 19%. That difference will only expand over time.

Latest Return

Since the last article was written, these shares have returned roughly -50%. That is largely due to the Iran crisis escalating oil prices. But a war (or whatever you want to call it) makes it exceedingly hard to tell what is going to happen in the future.

Note that the Iranian situation made for a big loss relatively quickly.

Furthermore, the professionals that are often needed to watch out for these events are often located in companies throughout the industry. These professionals will often do much better over time because they make money not only on the price of oil but also by providing needed services.

Commodity prices are a very low visibility industry. Future prices are very volatile as well. A fund like this could easily fail to return the expected profits because it resets every single day to restore the original structure of the fund. The last article noted this warning in the fund's prospectus.

Safety

I diversify by making baskets rather than by loading up on one idea (let alone using leverage). The issue with leverage is often that you have to get the timing right. Personally, I was never any good at timing the market.

Most of the great investors that I have followed talk about buying bargains and then selling when things are going very well. For oil, many would be selling now as it is highly unlikely that oil will stay over $100 for the long-term.

But many of the great investors sell without worrying about what the stock price does after they sell. Here, that is likely not the case as some serious profits could be missed. Here, timing the market is critical because the profits often happen all at one time. Buying a bargain and waiting for the (in this case) crude oil to move in the right direction can incur serious losses that could more than wipe out the profit opportunity when it happens.

Stocks Instead

Instead, I tend to look for managements where the company grows during both good and bad times. I then have a solid long-term profit without trying to time the market. Instead, I allow the pros at the company to deal with commodities like oil and I evaluate the results. Having an expert at a company working for me has always sounded like a far better deal than trying to do it myself. This puts a pro in charge of a situation rather than a mechanical ETF that I have really no qualifications to invest in.

Furthermore, most of the successful companies I follow in the oil and gas industry are successful without a whole lot of leverage (if any at all). I have a lot of requests to follow leveraged companies in the industry. You can see their tombstones under my profile for all the ones that are gone. Most were leveraged.

EQT (EQT) for example is a natural gas company with a darn good record since the Rice Brothers took over in a proxy battle before the Covid challenges. They have developed a cash generating juggernaut based upon the upstream business of natural gas production and the midstream business of transporting it to market. I would consider holding it until the story changes materially.

Headwater Exploration (CDDRF) is a smaller upstream company that produces heavy oil. It is probably one of the most profitable little upstream companies that I follow. Costs are low enough that it grows in both good and bad times. It is that rare company that pays a dividend while also growing.

Viper Energy (VNOM) this is a royalty company that collects its payments "off the top" with only a few expenses included. The stock price is down due to a secondary offering by selling shareholders that came after a merger. So, there is a fair amount of stock for the market to digest that was either issued for the merger or was sold by selling shareholders. But that will not last forever. The company also has a good record of growing its businesses.

Summary

The problem with any leveraged ETF is that a very clear pathway is needed (in this case down) to even think about an investment like this. Many times, that is not the case. If the timing of the investment is wrong by even a little bit, it can change the profitability of the outcome materially. That makes this ETF a strong sell most of the time.

Secondly, these ETF's often have a liquidation clause as part of the prospectus. Unfortunately for the investor, that liquidation often happens at a time when profitability follows and the fund is no longer around.

In many ways, these leveraged funds are like the lottery where few win. But that few winning often win big enough that many more are willing to lose money trying for that big win.

What usually happens instead is many investors leave the casino when they have no more money.

But that can be avoided by looking for well managed companies that survive the commodity business cycle to overall grow the business. Now an investor has professionals working for him rather than having to be the professional himself. A cyclical growth story can be held throughout the business cycle or purchased during the cyclical downturn while being sold during that cyclical recovery. Now timing is less important. The goal switches to buying a good enough bargain that the sale produces a decent profit (without waiting for the market top). That is a goal within the reach of many investors.

But since market timing is so critical, this ETF remains a strong sell because most investors will not in the long run make money with such a device. It is just better to look for opportunities where the odds are much more in favor of the investor.

Risks

It is possible that at some point the market will have a clear direction either up or down that would provide a profitable trading opportunity. But that possibility is such a rare opportunity, that it is not worth the time for most investors to even think about such a strategy. There are far more probable ways to make money.

It is also very possible that because this ETF resets on a daily basis that any potential investor may have to trade in and out of the fund on a daily basis. It may be particularly important to not have an overnight investment in a fund like this.

These considerations are why investing in a cyclical growth story is far more likely to lead to investment profits.

Here is the link to the SEC bulletin on leveraged ETFs, which you can access for better clarity: Updated Investor Bulletin: Leveraged and Inverse ETFs

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rapid Micro Biosystems: Big Pharma Adoption Driving Growth
data.description:
Thesis

Rapid Micro Biosystems, Inc. (RPID) has just released Q4 '25 and FY25 results. On the surface, we're seeing some small trends here, especially with the adoption of their main offering, the Growth Direct Platform. Naturally, as placements here grow, recurring revenue from consumables and service contracts is also expanding. The upside here for Rapid is that we see the Growth Direct platform becoming a widely adopted standard for microbial quality control, especially in larger pharmaceutical manufacturing companies. The large drug manufacturers are the key to faster growth here, in that they usually place multi-system orders, like the ones we just saw from Amgen and Samsung Biologics. However, the big caution here is that the company is still unprofitable and burning cash fast. Margins are negative, and we just saw a $47 million annual net loss. What makes it seem worse is that shares are trading at a premium.

So the growth story for RPID depends heavily on continued system placements and thus the follow-on orders from large pharmaceutical customers. This is why I've chosen to go with a Hold rating.

FY25/Q4 '25 financial overview

For Rapid, Q4 '25 revenue hit $11.3 million, which was about 37% year-over-year growth compared to the $8.2 million in the same quarter last year. I think we can put the growth mainly down to product sales, which we saw jump 78% to $9.3 million. So, there’s definitely some for their new system placements. The company also managed to place 16 Growth Direct systems during the quarter, which actually included a record multi-system order from Amgen.

These kinds of orders are what we want to see: enterprise-level adoption among larger biopharmaceutical manufacturers. Now, even though service revenue declined to just $2.0 million because of some timing differences in validation activities, their recurring revenue still grew about 10% to $4.6 million. This goes to show how their installed base can still generate ongoing consumables, service, and support revenue. As for profitability, even with the strong revenue growth, the net income line still remained pressured as their total cost of revenue hit $11.6 million, which gives us a negative gross margin of 3%. To put this in perspective, we saw a positive 12% margin in the prior-year quarter. But if we adjust for a one-time inventory write-off and the related warranty expenses, the company actually reported a non-GAAP adjusted gross margin of about 7%.

So there's still underlying operational progress here. However, the early-stage economics of scaling a complex hardware platform are taking their toll.

As for FY25, Rapid brought in $33.6 million in total revenue, which was about 20% growth when compared with 2024. What helped the year out was 28 system placements and 18 completed customer validations. On that note, product revenue jumped 25% to $23.4 million, whilst their service revenue increased 9% to $10.2 million. So overall, it was a decent, balanced expansion of both equipment sales and support services. Recurring revenue also managed to expand by about 15% up to $17.8 million. Again, even with the strong revenue trajectory, we saw Rapid continue to operate at a significant loss. Net loss for the year was $47.1 million. We can put the outflows here down to some hefty ongoing investments in commercialization, research and development, and operating infrastructure.

As for liquidity, they ended the year with about $39 million in cash and short-term investments, as well as $25 million of remaining borrowing capacity under its credit facility with Trinity Capital. If we take the total operating expenses in 2025 at about $81 million, that pro-forma cash position of about $64 doesn’t look too strong when you consider the company was at a loss last year.

In terms of guidance, management expects 2026 revenue to fall somewhere between $37 million and $41 million, along with another 30 to 38 system placements. Gross margins are projected at around 20%, which would imply some improvement in manufacturing efficiency. Management on the earnings call also highlighted decent customer momentum, including a meaningful multi-system order from Samsung Biologics that was received in early 2026. Again, we need to see more penetration among the larger biologics manufacturers.

How they make money

Before getting into what we want to see in 2026, I think it's worth going over their business model for those who aren’t too familiar with Rapid. They’re a life sciences technology company that develops automated systems which can then be used by pharmaceutical and biotechnology manufacturers, with the big se being for microbial quality control testing. The main product here is the Growth Direct System, which essentially just makes sure products like biologics, vaccines, and sterile injectables are free from contamination before they get released to market.

Now, all this is automated, which is their differentiating selling point compared to traditional laboratory testing processes that are typically manual and pretty slow. When they mention system orders, these are sales of the Growth Direct to pharmaceutical manufacturers, which is their big revenue driver; this is what we want to see ramp up. Elsewhere, they also get some recurring revenue from consumables, software, validation services, and some ongoing maintenance contracts tied to the systems they’ve previously installed. We often see multiple units ordered at once since other companies are trying to standardize the technology across all their manufacturing facilities. This also drives the growth case in that we need to see orders from bigger pharma companies, simply because they usually require more units due to their size.

The value of system orders

It’s definitely worth talking about the physical system orders since this is where revenue growth is going to come from. Q4 '25 was our best bullish indicator with revenue hitting a record $11.3 million. The revenue here mostly came from the Growth Direct systems placements. The company managed to install 16 systems for that quarter alone, compared with just 6 in the same quarter of 2024. So the global installed base is now up to 190 systems, of which we know that 155 have been validated and are fully operational.

As for product revenue, which includes both the physical system sales and consumables, this rose about 78% year over year to $9.3 million, which would suggest there is good adoption of the platform. Now, we also want to see recurring revenue growth, and on that note, consumables and service contracts grew about 15% for the full year to $17.8 million. This may not seem like a lot, but it made up about 53% of total revenue.

The key is that the bigger installed base they can get operational, the more revenue flow they’ll see from servicing and consumables that companies need to keep ordering. This is where I see stable, repeat revenue coming from, in that once they reach a certain number of installed systems, the recurring portion will be enough to maintain profitability.

Now, a lot of the big growth steps have been from larger multi-system orders from major pharmaceutical customers. They’re simply bigger and thus order more. We saw a record global rollout order from Amgen in Q4 '25 and also a new follow-on multi-system order from Samsung Biologics early this year. We also know that the company has previously disclosed some big customers, such as Lonza, so there’s definitely growing traction among the larger drug manufacturers. What makes these kinds of customers so valuable is that we often see them adopt the platform via a kind of land-and-expand strategy. Essentially, this means they might order/install a few systems early on and then expand deployments across their multiple facilities or production lines once they’re happy with the system.

For investors, the big thing to watch here is if the revenue stream truly scales in 2026. To justify the valuation, which we will see soon is quite expensive, we need continued growth in system placements, but also the follow-on orders from existing customers. So large multi-site rollouts similar to those seen with Amgen (AMGN) and Samsung (SSNLF).

MilliporeSigma partnership

Rapid has also announced a partnership with MilliporeSigma, which is the life science division of Merck KGaA. Now, this is pretty important in terms of their expansion strategy. As you know, MilliporeSigma has a pretty large global footprint in pharmaceutical/biotechnology manufacturing, which also comes with a massive distribution network. So what this does is give Rapid new access to a much broader customer base than it could realistically reach through its own direct sales team.

As per management, the collaboration comes with well-established customer demonstration laboratories in both Europe and Asia. think of this as a space where potential buyers can test and evaluate the Growth Direct system before committing to a purchase. The partnership is also tipped to support the company operationally, and since Rapid is exploring ways to leverage MilliporeSigma’s logistics infrastructure as well as the new supply chain capabilities, I can see this being a small driver in reducing manufacturing costs to help boost product margins. This would be particularly true for consumables. So, my thinking here is that they can expand global system placements via the partnership, but also improve profitability if they take advantage of the new platform.

Valuation

Rapid currently trades at a valuation which would appear quite high compared with its fundamentals. This is particularly true given that the company is still operating at significant losses. Now, because the firm has negative earnings and EBITDA, we should primarily value the company via revenue-based multiples. In that case, the stock trades at approximately 6.4x trailing price-to-sales and 5.2x trailing EV to sales. These multiples are also well above the healthcare sector median of about 3.5x sales.

So why the premium given that they’re generating only $33.6 million in 2025 revenue and continuing to report large net losses? I think the elevated valuation here is likely because of expectations that the company’s Growth Direct platform could achieve a much broader adoption across pharma manufacturing networks, with the key to steady income being long-term recurring revenue from consumables/services. However, in the near term, the stock is screening as somewhat overpriced relative to the current scale/profitability. We’re still seeing negative margins, ongoing cash burn, and a lot of uncertain timing for reaching operating leverage.

Looking ahead

As for what’s next, Rapid is actually showing a bit of innovation in that they have plans for a next-generation cloud-native software platform for its Growth Direct system. We can expect this to launch sometime in 2H26. The platform is tipped to basically be a complete redesign of the system’s software architecture, with the new intention being improved usability. As you can imagine, the overall machine has a learning curve for those set to use it. So Rapid’s aim here is to get easier integration with customers’ IT infrastructure. So naturally, that would come with secure cloud-based access to microbial testing data across manufacturing sites.

As is the trend in many industries, we can expect the new design to come with some kind of AI-driven analytics. I see this being particularly useful if companies are processing large volumes of microbial data. Again, the story here for Rapid is that we see the Growth Direct platform becoming a more widely adopted standard, especially in larger pharmaceutical manufacturing companies. This is what will help RPID with growth and later profitability.

data.title: Mission Produce Stock Is Cheap, But Risks Remain
data.description:
After the market closed on March 12th, the management team at Mission Produce, Inc. (AVO) announced financial results covering the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Unfortunately, shares of the company plummeted, trading down more than 8% at one point. This was in response to earnings per share falling short of expectations. However, revenue and adjusted earnings per share both came in above what analysts were hoping to see.

From a volume perspective, the company is doing exceptionally well. However, pricing for its products, most notably avocados, is proving to be a challenge for the business. In the past, I have taken a more neutral stance on the business. Without factoring in the after hours decline, shares of the company are up 0.8% since I reaffirmed it as a ‘hold’ candidate back in September of last year. That's not too bad considering that the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 2.6%.

To be honest with you, this is one prospect that I am on the fence about. On the one hand, the stock is not that expensive on either an absolute basis or relative to other similar firms. But on the other hand, market conditions are proving to be a challenge for the company. We also have some uncertainty associated with a major acquisition that management is making. That has the potential to significantly affect the overall trajectory of the company moving forward. Given these working parts, and the uncertainty that exists with this name, I think that taking the prudent approach and maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the appropriate course of action.

Mixed performance in these uncertain times

Probably the best place to start with when it comes to Mission Produce would be to touch on the most recent data that management has provided investors. That data covers the first quarter of 2026. During that time, revenue amounted to $278.6 million. That happened to be down 16.6% compared to the $334.2 million that the company reported a year earlier. This is a rather substantial drop, but it is worth noting that sales ended up coming in $17.9 million above what analysts were hoping to see.

All of the company’s decline in revenue came from its Marketing and Distribution segment. This is the largest part of the company and includes its activities when it comes to sourcing fruit from growers and then distributing it through its network. Overwhelmingly, this consists of avocados, which the business ultimately makes available to customers throughout North America, China, Europe, and even the UK. Sales for the segment plummeted from $295.8 million to $234.8 million. This plunge, according to the company, was in spite of the fact that the overall volume of avocados that it dealt with increased substantially. That number skyrocketed from 159.9 million pounds to 181.5 million pounds.

This rise was thanks to an abundant harvest in Mexico. The fact of the matter is that avocado supply in Mexico has been ballooning. According to one source, which came out in January of this year, an estimated 2.5 billion pounds of avocados are expected to be imported into the US during the current season. Mexico is a significant producer in the avocado space. In fact, its industry involves more than 200 importers, 91 Packers, and about 35,000 growers. Strong demand in the U.S. and other markets has fueled this overall expansion. As an example, thanks to favorable weather, improved farming practices, and other factors, we have seen the total avocado harvest in Mexico expand in recent years leading up to this point. From 2024 to 2025, as an example, the increase was from 2.67 million metric tons to 2.75 million metric tons.

Unfortunately, volume seems to be a little too high. I say this because while the company benefited from an increase in the amount of pounds that it dealt with, the average price per pound dropped from $1.75 to $1.22. This is because while end market demand is robust, the Mexican crop output is even larger. In fact, management anticipates that avocado industry volumes will rise by between 10% and 15% this year. But pricing is expected to be lower by between 30% and 35%.

One thing that is very interesting here is that management seems to be stuck between a rock and a hard place. They mentioned, as an example, that the lower pricing environment has led to a delayed start of the California harvest season. In essence, growers are waiting for market conditions to improve.

You would think that this would actually help to boost prices for the company. After all, lower supply means greater pricing power. However, management said that this has also reduced its ability to leverage its sourcing capabilities across other regions and has also resulted in lower asset utilization at its California packing facility. This actually risks pressuring the company in future quarters, with management specifically saying that the Marketing and Distribution segment should experience lower profitability in the second quarter of this year compared to the same time last year.

This is not to say that all aspects of the company suffered. Its Blueberries segment, as an example, reported revenue of $40.8 million. That is 12.1% above the $36.4 million the company reported the previous year. This rise was great to see in some respects. And it was the result of an increase in average per unit sales amounting to 9% year over year and to a 3% pop in volumes.

However, investors should not expect that growth to continue. I say this because management specifically pointed out the Peruvian blueberry harvest season as a pain point. They expect volume reductions from owned farms as farmers engage in pruning earlier and contend with unfavorable weather conditions. High costs that can be attributed to lower projected yields per hectare will certainly weigh on the space. This is also why segment profits limited from $7.6 million to $1.1 million.

On the bottom line, we saw some mixed results. The company went from a profit per share of $0.05 to a loss per share of $0.01. This ended up being $0.06 worse than what analysts were expecting. And it meant a decline in net profits from $3.9 million to negative $0.7 million. The good news is that at least adjusted profitability remained stable. It remained flat at $0.10 year over year, ultimately beating expectations by $0.03. This translated to a rise in adjusted net profits from $7.1 million to $7.3 million. But outside of that, profitability for the company worsened. Operating cash flow dropped from negative $1.2 million to negative $3 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $17.4 million to $11.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business fell from $21.8 million to $20.6 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. The general trend for the business has been increasing revenue leading up to this point, driven by a combination of greater pounds sold and higher average prices per pound. This year, however, we do see a real risk that revenue could end up being lower. The good news is that management is focused on long term growth opportunities. This includes continuing to expand into other product categories like mangoes and blueberries. But on top of this, we also have its pending acquisition of Calavo Growers (CVGW).

On January 14th of this year, management announced that they struck up a deal to acquire Calavo Growers in a transaction valued at $430 million on an enterprise value basis. This is a cash and stock deal, with shareholders of Calavo Growers expected to receive $14.85 per share in cash plus 0.979 shares of Mission Produce for each share of Mission Produce that they own. At the time that the deal was announced, this valued Calavo Growers at about $27 per share, which was 26.1% above its 30 trading day volume weighted average price of $21.41.

Conceptually, this transaction makes a great deal of sense. Throughout its lifetime, Calavo Growers has expanded to focus on value added prepared foods like guacamole, salsas, and dips. Value added offerings create a lot of potential, especially when it comes to margins, for companies in the food market that would otherwise be highly commoditized. And if everything goes according to plan, the combined business will still generate the vast majority of its revenue from avocados. In fact, about 84% of sales will come from those. Blueberries are a distant second place at 5%. But you also have both mangoes and guacamole at 4% each, followed by tomatoes at 2%.

The transaction, which is expected to close in August of this year, really grants Mission Produce the potential to capture a valuable piece of the space. For instance, the global avocado processing market is worth an estimated $2.7 billion. And from 2025 through 2033, it's expected to rise at a rate of about 8% annually.

The combined business should end up with annual EBITDA of $177 million. But that is based on the assumption that, within 18 months following the close of the transaction, management will capture about $25 million in annual run rate synergies. 48% of this will come from selling, general, and administrative costs. But we will also have large portions coming from packaging and distribution, freight, and sourcing activities.

Synergies are never guaranteed when it comes to a transaction like this. That's why it is important to evaluate a transaction both with and without synergies. In the chart above, I actually valued Mission Produce using results from both 2024 and 2025. You can see, in that chart, how the company is priced at the moment compared to five other similar firms. On a price to earnings basis and on a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the five firms that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, on an EV to EBITDA basis, two of the firms ended up being cheaper.

In the chart below, meanwhile, I compared it to Calavo Growers alone, with the current buyout terms that include synergies and no synergies. I also provided, in that chart, calculations for what the combined business should be worth, both with synergies and without them. In calculating this, I assumed a 6% interest rate on new net debt. I also used a 21% tax rate in my calculations.

What I found from this approach is that, if synergies aren't realized, Mission Produce is just a little worse off than it currently is from a valuation standpoint. But if synergies are realized, then the stock actually does look quite cheap. This is fantastic. But as I stated at the outset of this article, I remain concerned about pricing in this environment. A legitimate case could be made that continued strong harvests out of Mexico could compress profitability. And if that does occur, then the end result could be pain for the business in the near term.

Of course, if you do decide that you are a little less risk averse than I am when it comes to this name, the fact that the company is merging with Calavo Growers does create an interesting opportunity. In the table above, you can see what the hypothetical share price of Calavo Growers should be at different prices for Mission Produce. And in the chart below, you can see how much upside or downside investors would see based on movements in the share price of Mission Produce and assuming that its transaction does go through.

For those who are cautiously optimistic, perhaps acquiring Calavo Growers would be the better option here. You would see less downside of shares if Mission Produce fall from here. Consequently, you would also see less upside. But unless Mission Produce experiences a real surge in pricing, the amount that you would miss out on isn't all that significant.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my assertion that while Mission Produce is attractively priced at the moment, there are risk factors that need to be taken into consideration. There is no guarantee that its acquisition of Calavo Growers will go as intended. And the fact that the company will see its net debt increase $222.8 million because of this purchase does create some risk. Add on top of this the current state of the avocado market, with pricing plummeting as volumes increase, and I think that maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the appropriate course of action.

data.title: Rethinking Transatlantic Power: Why Trade Dependencies Cut Both Ways
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski | Ruben Dewitte | Julian Geib

Beyond American dominance

When it comes to trade relations and negotiations, the US is often portrayed as the superpower, using tariffs as threats to extract favourable outcomes. You could easily see this pattern during the first round of EU-US trade negotiations, which resulted more or less in damage control for Europe rather than a mutually beneficial agreement.

Following the Supreme Court ruling restricting President Trump’s use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) as well as new tariff threats during the Greenland conflict, uncertainty about new US trade actions resurfaced. Contrary to the negotiations last summer, the EU has taken a tougher stance, freezing ratification of the current deal and contemplating retaliatory trade actions.

Is this new stance simply a 'whistling in the dark' phenomenon, or does the EU have fundamental reasons to take a more aggressive stance vis-à-vis America?

Trade volume is not everything

The fact is that the US and EU remain deeply intertwined through trade. In 2025, the European Union was the largest foreign supplier to the United States, accounting for USD 633 billion in exports and 19% of total US imports, according to the US Census Bureau. In the other direction, the United States supplied around 14% of all extra‑EU imports, underscoring the depth of the transatlantic trade relationship.

However, economic leverage in trade does not so much stem from how much a country exports, but rather from how easily those exports can be replaced. Highly complex, scarce or strategically sensitive goods, such as critical raw goods, complex machinery or patent-protected pharmaceuticals, typically have a low short-term substitutability, so buyers cannot easily switch to alternative suppliers. This limited substitutability increases the economic cost of trade disruptions, thus raising the exporter’s bargaining power.

The notion of substitutability has several distinct dimensions, which we define and categorise in line with the approach developed by CEPII. Simply speaking, a country’s trade vulnerability depends on how many suppliers it relies on, whether it could switch to others globally or produce the good itself, and how long-lasting and strategically critical that dependence is. The fewer the alternatives and the more essential the sector – think chemicals, pharmaceuticals, steel, defence, transport or electronics – the greater the risk if supply chains break down.

Europe’s hidden leverage: Where global economies rely on the EU

Number of product lines where countries depend on EU suppliers (2024)

Key notes

Strategic important products are defined as the chemical, pharmaceutical, steel, defence, transport, and electronic products, where supply disruptions can have outsized economic and security implications.

Based on the CEPII methodology, we identify 468 EU and 462 US import dependencies out of 4400 product lines, of which respectively 129 and 119 are deemed strategically important. The EU’s exposure is heavily skewed towards China, which accounts for 63 strategic product dependencies (50% of all strategic dependencies), while US-based dependencies amount to only 17 products, or about 14% of the EU total.

By contrast, US dependencies are mainly split between China (48 products or 40%) and the EU (36 products or 30%). Based on this analysis, the US depends more on the EU (36 products) than the EU does on the US (17 products).

Within this landscape, country‑level heterogeneity is pronounced, with the three largest EU economies accounting for the majority of identified dependencies (22 out of 36). Germany stands at the centre, accounting for 13 of the 36 EU‑linked import‑dependency product lines. These dependencies are concentrated in areas that closely align with Germany’s industrial strengths: chemicals and pharmaceuticals.

Renewed tensions and tariff threats spark retaliatory discussion

New tensions resurfaced after President Trump threatened to “cut off all trade with Spain” following Madrid’s refusal to grant access to its military bases. At the same time, tariff uncertainty is rising again, with Section 122 rather than IEPPA in play for broad‑based tariffs under a strict 150‑day timeline, while Section 301 investigations were initiated just this week. EU lawmakers will reconvene on March 17 to decide whether to unfreeze the ratification process of the current US-EU trade agreement.

EU leaders also discussed the potential activation of the Anti‑Coercion Instrument (ACI), the EU’s so far unused “trade bazooka.” The ACI would allow Brussels to impose retaliatory measures, potentially including export controls, which could cause substantial economic damage.

Politically, however, the ACI is contentious: France is the loudest advocate of activating it, while Germany would be hit the hardest by any export-related retaliation due to its industrial structure and trade exposure. Or in other words, Europe would probably hit the US economy more by limiting its exports of advanced chemicals or pharmaceuticals rather than of wine and cheese. This asymmetry immediately raises questions about intra-EU compensation for companies affected by any export restrictions under the ACI. A topic that has not been discussed in public so far and which would be a clear test case for European solidarity if the ACI was ever activated.

A more assertive Europe?

The EU recently also strengthened its global trade footprint with the preliminary enforcement of the Mercosur trade agreement and the EU-India agreement. While both deals hold meaningful long-term potential and signalling power that Europe does not stand still, they are still far from matching the scale or economic weight of the US market.

In any upcoming discussions between the US and the EU, both sides should carefully consider how their economies are intertwined and how quickly this can turn into a point of friction, harming both sides. Any renewed trade tensions could easily spill over into other policy areas.

A closer look at the trade dependencies between both blocs shows that Washington has more to lose than is often assumed. At the same time, however, Europe should ensure that its tools are actually functional to avoid a trade bazooka suddenly turning into a water gun due to internal political constraints.

data.title: LDP: 7% Yield On Preferreds And Bonds
data.description:
Looking for more high-yield preferred stocks for your portfolio?

Many preferreds have been sitting at or above their call values over the past few months, but there are closed-end funds, "CEFs", such as the Cohen & Steers Ltd Dur Pref and Income Fund Inc (LDP), via which you can still gain preferred stock exposure.

Fund Profile:

"The primary investment objective of the Fund is to seek high current income through investment in preferred and other income securities. The secondary investment objective is capital appreciation. Preferred and other income securities are issued by U.S. and non-U.S. companies like banks, insurance companies, REITs, other diversified financials, as well as utility, energy, pipeline, and telecommunications companies." (LDP).

LDP incepted on 7/27/12. It held 264 positions, with $647M in Net Assets, and an average daily volume of 74K, as of 12/31/25. Management uses leverage, which was 32.37% as of 12/31/25, resulting in a 3.75% expense ratio, which includes interest expense.

Holdings:

Bonds comprised ~64% of the portfolio, preferreds were ~30%, and Convertibles were 5.5%, as of 12/31/25:

As of 12/31/25, within the Preferreds tranche, Institutional Preferred securities made up 88% of LDP's preferreds holdings, plus 7% in Floating Rate, and 5% in 5% in Retail Preferreds.

As of 12/31/25, Banking was LDP's largest sector exposure, at 56%, distantly followed by Insurance, at 12%, and Utilities, at 11%. It also has exposure to Pipelines, at 8%, plus Telecommunication Services, and Brokerage Services, both at 3%. In addition, there's a general "Other category which makes up 9% of the portfolio.

At 49%, the US is by far LDP's biggest country exposure, as other nations range from 3% for the Netherlands to 12% for Canada:

Credit quality includes 16% in investment grade securities, with non-investment grade comprising 69%, and non-rated securities comprising 12% of the portfolio:

LDP's bond holdings skewed more to long-term, with 15% in 15-20 year maturities, and 15.7% in 30+ year maturities.

As of 12/31/25, LDP's top 10 holdings comprised just 11.2% of its portfolio, running from 0.9% for an Enbridge 8.5% floating rate, due in 2084, to 1.5% for a Goldman Sachs Floating Rate Perpetual preferred:

Dividends:

LDP pays a monthly managed distribution and tends to go ex-dividend in the 2nd week of each month, with a ~ month-end payout date. At $20.50, LDPP yields 7.67%. It has paid $.1310 since July 2023, when it decreased its monthly payout from $.135.

Earnings:

NII rose 14% in the year ended 12/31/25. to $39M, while Net Realized Gains were down ~14%. The combination of NII and Net Realized Gains covered 2025 distributions by a solid 1.24X factor in 2025, vs. 1.19X in 2024.

Net Assets increased in both years, rising 6.6% in 2024 and by ~3% in 2025:

Taxes:

Management estimates that 7/31/25-12/31/25 distributions were comprised of 100% NII, while January/February 2026 distributions were still mostly NII, but also averaged ~10.7% Return of Capital, "ROC". ROC offers a tax deferral, but it reduces your tax basis, which will impact your tax liability if you sell your shares.

Performance:

LDP outperformed the Morningstar US CEF Preferreds category in 2023-2025, and retreated somewhat less in 2022:

As of 2/28/26, LDP had outperformed the category on a 3-, 5-, and 10-year basis. It also outperformed on an NAV basis over the past year, but lagged slightly on a price basis:

Risks:

Interest Rate Risk: As shown in the above chart, rising rates have a negative effect on LDP - it fell over 22% in 2022, a year of rising rates.Credit Default Risk: Since LDP invests in lower-rated securities, its portfolio has an increased risk of default than an investment-grade portfolio would have.Leverage: While leverage can increase profits on the way up, it can also exacerbate losses on the way down.

Valuations:

CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion.LDP was trading at a 7% discount to its daily NAV/share as of the 3/11/26 close, which was a somewhat larger discount than its 1-, 3-, and 5-year average discounts of 5.29%, 6.59%, and 4.92%, respectively.

Parting Thoughts:

LDP looks oversold on its slow stochastic chart, and is 11.5% above its 52-week low. It's down ~3% in price so far in 2026, and ~4% over the past month.

We advise waiting for a deeper discount - LDP's 52-week biggest discount was nearly 11%.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: IREN Limited: Massive ATM Filing Hammers The Bull Case (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Turning From "Buy" To "Hold" On The IREN Stock

Since my previous article on IREN Limited (IREN), which was a bullish upgrade from my end, the stock hasn't gone anywhere - just like the S&P 500 and NASDAQ (and most other major indices). It might look like IREN is setting up for a nice move up in the next weeks - at least technically. Plus, the recent rally in Nebius Group's (NBIS) stock may seem like a bullish confirmation for all neocloud investors that IREN will ultimately follow its peer.

While it may look like a clear buying opportunity - to grab some shares right at the 200-day moving average and wait for a breakout - I think IREN's bullish case has been hammered by the recent news that management decided to finance the firm's growth through a massive $6 billion ATM issuance. To put it in perspective, that $6 billion is over 43% of IREN's market cap:

They're purchasing GPUs with the issued capital, which is OK, but NBIS, for example, finances its growth through strategic partnerships with majors like Nvidia (NVDA), avoiding massive pressure on its current shareholders. The difference in corporate approaches has become more visible lately, and it's not in favor of IREN, in my opinion. Given all this, I decided to downgrade IREN to "Hold", although it might look like the stock is setting up for a bounce (technically).

Why Do I Think So?

As I usually do, I'd like to take a look at the firm's latest financial results first to have a deeper understanding of how the business has been doing lately. IREN published its fiscal Q2 2026 results in early February, showing $184.7 million in sales (-23% QoQ), missing the consensus by over 17%, according to Seeking Alpha. It's not the main bearish factor, of course, because IREN is still clearly in the process of pivoting from Bitcoin (BTC) mining toward neocloud-related revenues. But the size of the miss looks concerning to me. Anyway, on the bottom line, the deterioration looked even more alarming as IREN's adjusted EBITDA dropped from $91.7 million in Q1 to just $75.3 million in Q2 because of the transition costs, and it pressed the EPS way more meaningfully than analysts had been expecting initially.

IREN recorded unrealized losses of over $219 million for the quarter, which were related to prepaid forwards and capped calls of the convertible notes.

The good thing is that the AI cloud business is soaring rapidly - in Q2, it went up by 136% on a QoQ basis. In absolute terms, I think it still doesn't look like something meaningful - that $17.3 million is just a mere fraction of the total revenue pie, so the low base helps IREN report massive QoQ and YoY growth rates quarter after quarter. Again, I think it's totally fine to have this low base initially, but I would think twice before calling the QoQ doubling in their AI cloud business something extraordinary.

The management's guidance sounded reassuring as IREN confirmed that they're targeting $3.4 billion in ARR by the end of 2026 on a 140,000 GPU deployment.

We have multiple advanced negotiations underway for larger scale deployments and are also seeing hyperscalers and AI enterprises increasingly focus on air-cooled GPUs given the faster deployment time lines. Operationally, execution is tracking well across the portfolio, and we expect to deliver 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026, positioning us to deliver $3.4 billion in annualized run rate revenue.

Source: IREN's Q2 2026 earnings call

After the Q2 release, they expanded this target to $3.7 billion following the purchase of >50,000 Nvidia B300 GPUs (thus, the planned fleet has increased to 150,000 GPUs).

The extensive expansion is clearly not going anywhere. Last week, IREN filed an updated 424B5 prospectus, expanding the ATM offering capacity to $6 billion. But what we haven't heard on the call was the concrete confirmation that IREN would hit its original calendar Q1 deadline for the $500 million ARR - the management seemed to shift the market's focus to a much larger, longer-term target of $3.7 billion in ARR by the end of 2026.

Investors who own IREN are convinced that a new equity ATM filing equivalent to almost half of their current market cap…means another deal is imminent. You know, like a material event that would need to be disclosed concurrent with this equity filing.

But never fear, in true IREN style, they (sort of) raised ARR guidance for the December 2026 quarter, but neglected to use this opportunity to reaffirm the $500M digital revenue ARR guidance for the quarter we are currently in.

Source: James Chanos on X

If the execution of smaller-scale promises fails, or the shorter-term plans are delayed/shifted to the longer-term plans, it can create a massive risk for IREN shareholders, especially when we recall the massive $6 billion ATM issuance.

First, investors are basically paying upfront for the capacity that remains at risk of being delayed again. The delays are priced in only for the current fiscal year - the "shift to the long-term" has only resulted in higher forward revenue estimates, based on Seeking Alpha.

Second, the issuance itself creates a massive addition to the float supply - the absence of sufficient buyers could lead to a significant sell-off and repricing to the downside.

I understand that IREN needs to raise capital to finance its future growth - that's what all hyper-growth entities do at this business cycle. But the proposed ATM program allows the sale of newly issued shares directly into the open market at current trading prices, and so the current shareholders aren't protected in any way. At the very same time, with the very same need for capital raise, we see Nebius, which partnered with NVDA, securing a direct $2 billion investment - this move basically isolated all NBIS shareholders from open-market sales and resulting pressure ahead. That's why I think IREN could have done it better - we've already seen them securing other forms of capital over the last few months, like customer prepayments, convertible notes, etc. It looks like the demand from institutional investors may be hitting limits, and so IREN has decided to sell directly to the public to access the much-needed capital - this looks like a red flag to me.

Given all the above points, I think IREN shouldn't trade with today's massive premium in the price-to-sales ratio relative to NBIS or even CoreWeave (CRWV) - these two can seemingly avoid diluting their investors at IREN's pace.

At a 3-4x price-to-sales 1-year forward ratio, IREN should correct from today's price levels by over 25%, which is a strong reason for me to downgrade it to at least "Hold".

The Bottom Line

Most of my arguments might sound too bearish for IREN - then why am I downgrading it to "Hold" and not "Sell"? It's a reasonable question. First, I like this IT niche in general - neoclouds are solving the issues of scarcity of electrical power and infrastructure for major clients, so IREN's offerings/services should stay in demand and eventually bring some positive returns on invested capital. Second, I still like the forward sales growth - I think IREN's implied P/S ratio should be lower than it is today, but if one is willing to hold the stock for at least 3 years, IREN should become reasonably valued at the end.

The risk-to-reward setup in IREN doesn't look sufficiently bullish to me right now, mainly because of the ATM offering and the concerns that I have regarding the speed/quality of their business transition. Hence, my downgrade to "Hold".

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Microsoft: SaaS/Cloud Winner With Rule Of 37.3% Outperformance
data.description:
I previously covered Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) (MSFT:CA) in January 2026, discussing why the stock was an even better buy then, thanks to the cheaper valuations/double-digit upside potential arising from the prior correction.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am upgrading the MSFT stock as a Strong Buy here, thanks to their robust Enterprise SaaS/cloud monetization cadence, their well-covered, multi-year data center spending plans, the cheaper valuations from the recent SaaSpocalypse fears, and the great insights from their established trading pattern since 2020.

MSFT Proves SaaS/Cloud Spending Remains Resilient

Since my last Buy rating, MSFT has underperformed with a -14% correction against the wider market at -1%, with a similar development also observed in its SaaS peers in varying degrees.

SaaSpocalypse Fears Overly Done

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to the prior SaaSpocalypse fears, as Anthropic (ANTHRO) launches Claude Cowork - "autonomous AI agents that perform tasks without humans in the loop in particular," with it presenting the risks of AI potentially disrupting the enterprise SaaS' per-seat monetization prospects.

At a time when AI is seen as a potential SaaS disruptor, it is unsurprising then that the market has decided to de-risk first while contributing to the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF's (IGV) selloff by -35.3% between the 52-week highs and lows.

I am of the opinion that MSFT's similarly steep meltdown by -30.7% between the 52-week highs and the February 2026 bottom has been overdone indeed, since their Enterprise SaaS monetization trends through the Productivity and Business Processes segment remain robust.

The same has been observed in their growing Productivity and Business Processes revenues to $34.11B in FQ2'26 (+15.9% YoY), the expanding gross margins of 82% (+0.9 points YoY), and the notably richer operating margins of 60.3% (+3 points YoY/+21 from FY2019 levels of 39.3%).

These numbers exemplify MSFT's excellent pricing power and the stickiness of their Enterprise SaaS offerings, as observed in the higher adoption of the paid M365 commercial offerings at over 450M seats (+6% YoY).

The same has been observed in the Microsoft 365 Copilot at over 15M seats, with seat adds growing by +160% YoY and daily active users expanding by 10x YoY, despite the relatively nascent launch in September 2023.

The growing seats and higher ARPUs directly contributed to the drastically growing commercial Remaining Performance Obligation [RPO] of $625B (+109.7% YoY) and the RPOs' weighted average duration of approximately 2.5 years as well.

I believe that these metrics offer interested investors a great insight into MSFT's excellent SaaS monetization prospects despite the prior SaaSpocalypse fears.

While much of their AI/Copilot capabilities may rely on OpenAI's (OPENAI) GPT‑5.4 platform and MSFT has yet to develop a competitive AI platform to match competitors, including Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude, I am not overly concerned for now.

This is because "OpenAI remains Microsoft’s frontier model partner and Microsoft continues to have exclusive IP rights and Azure API exclusivity until Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)" sometime in 2032, while "includes models post-AGI, with appropriate safety guardrails."

With a few years ahead before the partnership exclusivity ends, readers may still patiently await MSFT Superintelligence Team's future breakthrough beyond their current reliance on OpenAI.

Capex Spending Is Necessary

Given the drastically growing commercial RPOs as discussed above, I believe that MSFT's YTD capex of $49.27B (+60.3% YoY) remains reasonable here.

This is especially since MSFT continues to report an expanding free cash flow [FCF] generation of $31.54B (+21.3% YoY) and a richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $49.2B (+54.5% YoY), compared to other hyperscaler peers, including:

  • Amazon (AMZN) at FCF of $11.19B in FY2025 (-70.7% YoY) and net cash of $57.38B (+18.1% YoY),
  • GOOG at $73.26B in FY2025 (inline YoY)/net cash of $80.29B (-5.2% YoY), and
  • Oracle (ORCL) at LTM FCF of -$24.73B (-525% YoY)/net debt of $95.47B in FQ3'26 (-17.3% YoY), respectively,

... with it apparent that MSFT remains more than well capitalized to engage in their aggressive data center-related capex trends, as observed in the growing FCF and the richer balance sheet.

Given that "our customer demand continues to exceed our supply," I am of the opinion that MSFT's plans to take up ORCL's and OpenAI's previously planned expansion in Abilene, Texas, are prudent as well, after the latter two "decided not to expand their data center" lease.

This is especially since numerous hyperscalers/neoclouds have reiterated the insatiable compute demand in the recent Q4'25 earnings season, with it explaining the necessarily growing data center-related spending trends entering 2026.

This is on top of market analysts already projecting the global data center capacity to "nearly double from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030," with MSFT's growing capex spends likely to be accretive to their Microsoft Cloud growth prospects beyond the +25.9% YoY reported in FQ2'26 along with their prospective cloud share gains beyond the 21% reported in Q4'25 (inline YoY).

These developments naturally underscore that MSFT's recent AI-related selloff has been unwarranted, given their robust Enterprise SaaS prospects and their well-covered, multi-year data center growth plans, albeit with it also triggering a compelling dip-buying opportunity for those who have been patient.

MSFT's Profitable Growth Trends Are Cheaply Valued

As a result of MSFT's growing YTD revenues of $158.94B (+17.5% YoY) and adj. EPS of $8.27 (+26.6% YoY), along with the management's FQ3'26 revenue guidance of +16% YoY, it is unsurprising that the consensus has already raised their forward estimates.

This is with MSFT expected to report excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.1%/+17.9% through FY2028, with it building upon their historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +14.5%/+18.8% and a 10Y CAGR of +11.7%/+17.9%, respectively.

Thanks to the -27.1% correction from the 52-week highs, MSFT now looks a lot more compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 9.28x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 24.47x, down from the 1Y mean of 10.89x/30.21x, the 5Y mean of 11x/31.59x, and the 10Y mean of 8.51x/27.96x, respectively.

This is especially since MSFT has already achieved the YTD Rule of 37.3% outperformance (+4.3 points YoY), based on the YTD revenue growth of +17.5% YoY (against a year ago at +14% YoY) and the YTD free cash flow margins of 19.8% (against a year ago at 19% YoY).

For reference, the "Rule of 40" is a metric that I often use to measure "a SaaS company's growth rate when added to its free cash flow rate should equal 40 percent or higher," with it indicative of the management "knowing which levers to pull and which targets to aim for is especially important in SaaS because of the lag between bookings and revenues, the upfront expense of acquiring customers, and the constant rate of R&D spend required to keep features and products current."

When comparing MSFT's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 9.28x and YTD Rule of 37.3% outperformance to its diversified peers, including:

  • hyperscaler - AMZN at 2.90x/13.5% (FY2025 revenue growth by +12% YoY and FCF margins of 1.5%),
  • hyperscaler - GOOG at 7.81x/33% (FY2025 revenue growth by +15% YoY and FCF margins of 18.1%),
  • hyperscaler/enterprise SaaS - ORCL at 8.69x/-26% (LTM revenue growth by +14% YoY and LTM FCF margins of -40%),
  • enterprise analytics SaaS - Palantir (PLTR) at 48.94x/107% (FY2025 revenue growth by +56% YoY and FY2025 FCF margins of 51%),
  • enterprise creative SaaS - Adobe (ADBE) at 4.29x/52.4% (FY2025 revenue growth by +11% YoY and FY2025 free cash flow margins of 41.4%), and
  • enterprise analytics/workflow SaaS - Snowflake (SNOW) at 10.03x/54% (FY2026 revenue growth by +29% YoY and FY2026 FCF margins of 25%), respectively,

I believe that MSFT remains reasonably valued here, thanks to the cheaper valuations from the recent selloff and the robust SaaS, profitable growth metrics.

This is especially since MSFT is a lot cheaper at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.36x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 24.47x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +17.9%, nearer to the sector median of 1.29x while moderated from the 5Y mean of 2.37x and the 10Y mean of 1.56x.

A similar conclusion may also be derived when comparing against AMZN at 1.55x, GOOG at 2.10x, ORCL at 1.03x, PLTR at 2.19x, ADBE at 1.06x, and SNOW at 2.55x, with it apparent that the meltdown has triggered an improved risk/reward profile for those looking to add MSFT, a leading SaaS/cloud stock, after the prior SaaSpocalypse/AI fears.

MSFT Is A Great, Strong Buy Here

For now, MSFT's meltdown is seemingly over as well, as observed in the stock being well defended at the $380s by February 23, 2026 along the established uptrend support line since March 2020.

The same oversold position has been observed in MSFT's low RSIs and trading volumes by the time of writing, with it implying that the worst may already be behind us.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 24.47x (near to the sector median of 21.76x) and the annualized H1'26 adj EPS of $16.54 (+26.6% YoY), it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value estimates of $404.70.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $22.34, there remains an excellent upside potential of +35% to my long-term price target of $546.60 as well, as the stock trades along its historical up/downtrend trading cycle anchored by the established uptrend support line since March 2020.

As a result of their excellent Enterprise SaaS/cloud monetization cadence, their well-covered, multi-year data center spending plans, the cheaper valuations from the recent meltdown, and the great insights from their established trading pattern, I am upgrading the MSFT stock as a strong buy here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Eurozone Industrial Outlook Thrown Into Uncertainty As Energy Woes Return
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands

While the European Commission is making progress in implementing the Industrial Accelerator Act, the conflict in the Middle East is currently clearly serving as an industrial decelerator, as higher energy prices and broader supply chain disruptions are putting the brakes on the much-anticipated manufacturing recovery.

And today’s release of January production data already shows that recent optimism among manufacturers has not exactly been justified by production data. The decline of -1.5% in January follows a drop of -0.6% in December. While production had been elevated compared to 2024 levels for much of last year, the January level of industrial production is now the lowest since December 2024.

It has to be said that a large drop in Irish production, whose data is notoriously volatile, contributed significantly to the decline in January, but the misery was shared more broadly as Germany, Italy and Spain all noted declines compared to December.

Stronger production hopes hinge on the expectation that increased public investment in defence and infrastructure will boost manufacturing output. But the risk is that another prolonged surge in energy costs will shatter hopes of a recovery in energy-intensive industries, which have already been struggling since the previous shock in energy prices in 2021-22. While other sectors have managed to thrive despite the headwinds from the previous shock, this could still be enough of a drag on manufacturing to pour cold water on hopes of a manufacturing recovery.

So just as optimism had returned to manufacturing, geopolitics is clearly putting downside risk back on the table.
