data.title: BGC Group: FMX Expansion Is Transforming The Business, But Expectations Are Already High
data.description:
My thesis

In my view, the current valuation suggests that investors are already pricing in significant success of the FMX platform and the company’s strategic transformation. BGC Group, Inc. (BGC) ended the 2025 financial year having reached record revenues of $2.94 billion, which is a 30% growth compared to last year. This increase happened because of organic 14.9% revenue growth across all asset classes and OTC Global Holdings integration, after which the company became the world's largest energy broker.

The biggest part of the strategy still remains the FMX Futures Exchange platform, which at the end of 2025 captured 40% of the market share of US treasury bond net transactions. Fenics also continues to increase the efficiency of its activities. The company's overall debt is reaching $1.2 billion, while the cash reserve makes up $851.5 million.

The current P/E rate reaching around 29x suggests that the market's valuation is based on high expectations for FMX's ability to take away liquidity from CME Group. Investors have to assess the risk that in 2026 the planned $25 million cost savings could be absorbed by the continued technological investments, which are mandatory to fight competitors in the futures market, so the 21.3% adjusted profit margin remains the main stability indicator.

Business overview

BGC Group is one of the biggest wholesale financial market intermediaries in the world, providing services to institutional clients, such as investment banks, risk insurance funds, and governments. The company operates as a bridge between the buyers and the sellers in interest rate, foreign exchange, credit, equity, and commodity markets.

In 2025, the company reached record numbers financially, where yearly revenues grew 30% and reached $2.94 billion. A significant boost was given by the energy, raw materials, and shipping (ECS) segment, which, after the successful acquisition of OTC and its integration, became the largest energy broker in the world, generating 33% of the total revenue.

The business is diversified, though the main business activity is focused on the EMEA region, making 53% of the revenue, and also in the US market with a 36% share. The business model is based on commissions for executing transactions, and the efficiency of the activities is measured by the income generated per broker, which in 2025 reached $1.18 million. The company's market capitalization currently reaches $4.3 billion, while the P/E ratio is around 29x, reflecting the market's expectations for further technological expansion and the market share growth in the treasury securities segment.

Strategic shift

BGC Group is carrying out a strategic shift, reshaping the traditional voice mediation model into a highly profitable electronic platform managed by the Fenics brand. This change is the main factor for the margin expansion, because electronic trading platform revenue at the end of 2025 grew 15.4%, well over the overall market average.

The most significant rotation element is FMX Futures’ exchange activity, which directly competes with CME Group in the U.S. Treasury bond and SOFR futures markets. At the end of 2025, FMX UST platform occupied a record 40% net treasury bond market share, while SOFR futures contract volume in the last quarter jumped 82%.

This migration to fully electronic systems allows the company to implement a cost reduction program, whose goal is to save $25 million over the year in 2026, decreasing dependency on the expensive broker staff. Product rotation also includes data and analytics services expansion, striving to create recurring revenue streams, which lowers business sensitivity to cyclical market activity fluctuations. The switch to integrated solutions, including cash, future trades, and derivatives in one ecosystem, positions the company as a full-fledged exchange operator, not just a traditional broker.

Valuation

BGC Group's share price is currently around $9.30, which reflects the market's valuation after the record results in 2025. The company's revenues in the last year grew 30%, which was led by the integration of OTC Global Holdings as well as the growth of the electronic trading platform Fenics.

The company's forward P/E is reaching 7-8x, though the GAAP P/E rate is over 29x, because of $54.8 million in one-off restructuring costs. The main value engine is the FMX platform, which occupied 40% of the US treasury bond market share. Even though the EV/EBITDA rate of 15.5x suggests a technological premium, the free cash flow yield is reaching 10.5%. Dividend yield remains low, 0.9%, because capital is directed to FMX infrastructure. Current valuation shows that the market recognizes the market model switch, though it remains cautious due to a possible slowdown in trading activity.

Comparison

BGC Group shares are currently traded with a forward P/E of 7-8x, which is not too far off from the main competitor, TP ICAP Group (TCAPF), with its 7.6x rate. Though the main difference comes out when valuing the P/B ratio, where BGC Group's is reaching 4.4, while TP ICAP's P/B ratio is just 1.0.

Such disproportion suggests that investors value BGC assets as way more expensive for the successful FMX platform's expansion and the bigger operational efficiency, even though both companies have a similar forward P/E. For comparison, Compagnie Financière Tradition (CFNCF) is traded with a 15.5x forward P/E and 3.7 P/B ratios, which shows the market's confidence in its more stable, but slower business model growth. Meanwhile, CME Group, which is a direct BGC competitor in the treasury bond futures market, maintains a 26.6x forward P/E, highlighting a huge gap between exchange operators and brokers.

BGC's goal of 18% ROE justifies a higher P/B ratio compared to TP ICAP, whose ROE is not even reaching 9%. These numbers confirm that BGC is successfully transforming from a pure voice intermediary to a technology financial infrastructure company, though it still isn't able to reach the valuation level of full-fledged exchanges.

Risks

BGC Group's risks currently remain directly tied to FMX's future exchange's ability to withstand CME Group's competition. Though FMX, until the end of 2025, had a 40% share in the US treasury markets, pulling liquidity into SOFR futures trades requires constant financial incentives, which limit profitability.

If trading volumes, which at the end of 2025 grew 82%, stabilize or even drop, investments into the platform will turn negative. Regulation changes in the US related to mandatory central clearing increase compliance costs and operational complexity.

The company's financial stability depends on the $25 million cost savings plan's success, because staff costs still make up around 60% of the revenues. With $1.2 billion in overall debt and a 1.34 debt-to-equity ratio, BGC Group has no room for error when the interest rates are high. Revenue concentration in the US and EMEA markets and the dependency on market fluctuations mean that the decreasing macroeconomic uncertainty will directly lower the broker's revenues. The current market's valuation has already priced in aggressive growth, so any FMX expansion lag could cause a sudden decline in the share price.

Thesis summary

BGC Group is currently experiencing a transformation period, and the company is gradually moving away from the traditional voice mediation model to the technology based infrastructure of electronic platforms. Record revenues in 2025 and strong growth show that the strategy is starting to give results, especially after OTC Global Holdings integration and the fast Fenics and FMX platform development. FMX futures market expansion is becoming the main long term engine for growth, because the company is trying to take away part of the liquidity from the market leaders, such as CME Group.

However, in my view, the current valuation already reflects the high expectations from the FMX platform's success and the further business model expansion. Though BGC Group is generating strong revenue growth and is improving business efficiency, investors need to evaluate the risk of competitiveness, large technological investments, and the possible cyclicality of trading activity in financial markets. That is why further share price dynamics will mostly depend on whether the company manages to maintain FMX's share of the market, efficiently lower costs, and further expand revenue generated by electronic platforms.

data.title: Oracle: The AI Infrastructure Juggernaut Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (ORCL) is in the middle of a massive bet as it's pouring capital into AI cloud infrastructure at a pace that would have been unthinkable a few years ago. ORCL’s Q3 fiscal 2026 earnings backed up the story with 22% YoY revenue growth, 84% cloud infrastructure growth, and a 325% jump in remaining performance obligations (RPO) to $553 billion. The part that has the market worried is that ORCL is borrowing heavily to reimagine the future of its company. ORCL’s long-term debt has ballooned to $124.72 billion, and it is generating -$24.74 billion in free cash flow (FCF). I’m bullish on ORCL for the long term because I think its cloud infrastructure spending will drive serious cash from operations growth over the next two to three years, just like we saw with the hyperscalers before it. This is not a set-it-and-forget-it type of investment, as its data center delivery, debt management, and RPO conversion all have to go right. If ORCL doesn’t deliver on these metrics, the stock is likely to retrace to the downside. I believe that the investment case in ORCL needs years to play out, not months. Based on what the other hyperscalers have produced, I feel that ORCL’s future earnings will be strong, and this is an investment that could generate significant alpha in the future.

Oracle Showed the Market Why Their CapEx Allocations Are the Future of Their Company

ORCL’s cloud infrastructure revenue hit $4.9 billion in Q3, which was an increase of 84% YoY. The real story here is that Oracle cloud infrastructure has accelerated its YoY growth in the 2026 fiscal year from 55% in Q1 to 68% in Q2 to 84% in Q3. In the first 9 months of the 2026 fiscal year, Oracle cloud infrastructure revenue generated $12.3 billion, which exceeded the $10.2 billion generated in the entire 2025 fiscal year. That kind of acceleration makes me believe that ORCL is making the right call on capital allocation. The key driver is AI workloads, as ORCL has discussed that demand for cloud computing for AI training and inferencing continues to grow faster than supply. ORCL has become a go-to infrastructure partner for some of the world’s biggest AI model developers because they offer GPU clusters at competitive price-performance ratios that often undercut the hyperscale incumbents. ORCL is focused on its multi-cloud database, which has seen its revenue scale 531% YoY, which is an indication that demand is exceptionally strong for its workloads that work across Azure, AWS, and Google Cloud.

ORCL’s SaaS business grew 13% YoY to $4 billion, with Fusion Cloud ERP up 17% and NetSuite Cloud ERP up 14%. ORCL continues to build more SaaS applications for its customers at a lower cost due to AI-powered code generation, which management believes will expand its addressable market while improving margins. ORCL’s Cloud Revenue, which includes infrastructure and software as a service, came in at $8.9 billion, which was up 44%, while its total revenue came in at $17.2 billion, which was up 22% YoY. ORCL’s remaining performance obligations increased 325% YoY to $553 billion. Oracle cloud infrastructure is driving the lion’s share of ORCL’s revenue growth right now. Their cloud revenue now accounts for 52% compared to 48% last quarter, and that revenue mix shift is the central thesis for Oracle bulls. ORCL is moving away from its old license/support model and into a high-growth recurring cloud revenue stream.

ORCL is playing catch-up with CapEx as they have now allocated $48.25 billion in the TTM. This is an increase of 127.43% YoY and 2,159.95% since the 2021 fiscal year. ORCL is still well behind Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) from a CapEx allocation perspective, but they are closing the distance quickly. What I look at is the correlation between CapEx allocation and cash from operations growth. It looks like MSFT, AMZN, and GOOGL have witnessed big cash from operations growth as more data centers came online to fulfill their RPO. The interesting part is that ORCL’s cash from operations growth has trailed its peers by a wide margin, and I believe it is because they were late to the game with deploying CapEx. Since 2021, AMZN has increased its cash from operations by 201.15%, while MSFT’s has grown 109.16%. ORCL, on the other hand, has increased by 48.01%. ORCL is generating $23.51 billion in cash from operations, while MSFT is producing $160.51 billion, and AMZN is generating $139.51 billion. I believe that ORCL’s cash from operations will have its own growth spurt over the next two years as they catch up on the CapEx outlay. If the hyperscalers have taught us anything, it is that the cash from operations trails CapEx investments. I think the market is missing this.

The amount of CapEx that ORCL is spending has the market torn. ORCL is spending $50 billion in planned CapEx for fiscal year 2026 compared to its operating cash flow of $23.51 billion. They’re burning more than double what they bring in, and their FCF is in the negative at -$24.74 billion. Unlike MSFT or AMZN, ORCL can’t just absorb that hit because its cash generation is a fraction of what they're able to produce. ORCL’s entire financial picture depends on this CapEx actually turning into revenue on or ahead of schedule so they can monetize their RPO. If there are delays getting data centers online and generating returns, the pressure on the balance sheet will intensify as the carrying costs on their debt continue to increase. I’m comfortable with the risk because I think the payoff is coming, but the reality is that this adds a layer of volatility that investors need to size appropriately.

ORCL’s Debt Load is the Elephant in the Room

I think ORCL’s debt is the single biggest variable in its investment case. ORCL has constructed a plan to raise between $45 and $50 billion during 2026, which will come from debt and equity. On the debt side, they went to market with a $25 billion bond offering in early February. It was an eight-part deal led by Goldman Sachs, and the order book hit $129 billion. What worries me more is the rolling nature of the debt. ORCL is taking on new obligations at today’s rates while older, cheaper debt matures and eventually needs to be refinanced at higher costs. Total long-term debt jumped 24.74% in a single quarter to $124.72 billion. The debt-to-equity ratio dwarfs its hyperscale peers.

On the equity side, ORCL set up a $20 billion at-the-market program through Citigroup Inc. (C) to sell up to 121.5 million shares at market prices. That dilutes current shareholders, but it’s the trade-off management made to protect the investment-grade rating. They also said they won’t issue more bonds beyond the $50 billion amount in calendar year 2026. In order to make this seem reasonable, I believe that management was careful to highlight that for the large AI contracts, a lot of the equipment is funded through customer prepayments or customer-supplied GPUs. This is important because it takes some CapEx off ORCL’s books. In the short term, this doesn’t solve the issue of generating $23.5 billion in operating cash flow against $50 billion in CapEx. ORCL is continuously needing to raise capital, and it's getting more expensive as the interest expenses continue to expand.

Oracle’s Valuation is Very Interesting at These Levels

When I line up ORCL’s forward earnings against AMZN, GOOGL, MSFT, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Apple Inc. (AAPL), and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), it looks cheap. ORCL is expected to generate $7.44 of EPS this year, $7.94 next year, and $10.72 the year after that. ORCL is currently trading at 21.92 times this year’s earnings and 15.22 times 2028 earnings. ORCL has 44.09% EPS growth on the horizon over the next 2 fiscal years. ORCL is trading at the 2nd lowest forward valuation out of these companies, with the third largest amount of EPS growth. From an earnings perspective, I think that ORCL looks inexpensive, especially given what the next several years of profitability could look like as they transform RPO to straight revenue. I think that the market is overlooking ORCL’s earnings potential because of its debt load, and this could be a costly mistake over the next several years.

What Management is Telling Us About the Road Ahead

The forward guidance from the Q3 call gives us a useful roadmap. For Q4 in fiscal year 2026, management expects total revenue to grow 19% to 21% in USD, with cloud revenue growing 46% to 50%. They guided non-GAAP EPS to $1.96 to $2.00. For the full fiscal year 2026, the company stuck with its $67 billion revenue target and $50 billion CapEx plan; no changes there. The big number that I am watching out for is the $90 billion of revenue that management believes they will generate next year. That’s roughly 34% growth YoY, which could become a narrative changer since ORCL is currently doing roughly $67 billion in revenue. ORCL is focusing on its future footprint to make its revenue guidance a reality. The Abilene, Texas campus is roughly a 1,000-acre site with eight data centers. Management said on the Q3 call that two buildings are already running workloads on Nvidia GB200 Blackwell racks, and the remaining buildings are scheduled for completion by mid-2026. This will bring the total capacity to about 1.2 gigawatts. In addition to this site, ORCL has committed to building 4.5 gigawatts of AI data center capacity for OpenAI across several U.S. sites, which include campuses near Detroit and in Wisconsin. In Q3, ORCL delivered more than 400 megawatts of capacity to its customers, and 90% was delivered on time or early.

ORCL’s CEO, Clay Magouyrk, discussed margins on the Q3 call, which provided some encouraging information. He indicated that the AI capacity delivered in Q3 came in at a 32% gross margin, which exceeded their guidance of 30%. He made sure to discuss the fact that as ORCL’s margins expand, its fixed costs get spread across a larger revenue base, which causes profitability to improve. It was also discussed that their multi-cloud expansion will have 33 live regions with MSFT, 14 with GOOGL, and 8 with AWS, which is expected to increase to 22 by the end of Q4. This is an indication that there is strong demand for their multi-cloud. Even though management is signaling that they can generate $90 billion of revenue in 2027, the reality is that ORCL needs to keep converting RPO into revenue while delivering data centers on time. This allows them to keep their customers locked in, but if they miss this target, then I could see shares of ORCL declining as the market knows how much leverage is behind it.

Risks to Investing in ORCL

The biggest risk to ORCL is the balance sheet. ORCL has $38.46 billion in cash and another $677 million in marketable securities, but its long-term debt is $124.72 billion. That’s a net liquidity position of -$85.59 billion. Their CapEx exceeds their cash from operations by so much that FCF is -$24.74 billion. If the AI buildout stalls or demand shifts, ORCL is the most exposed name in the group because it is not funding this from profits the way its peers are. It’s important to note the debt dynamics because the $25 billion bond deal was priced at a premium to peers, and the market knows ORCL is sitting on over $100 billion in debt with negative free cash flow. As older, cheaper bonds mature, they’ll be replaced with new debt at today’s rates. That means interest expense is likely to increase even if ORCL doesn’t borrow another dollar. If ORCL’s revenue doesn’t ramp the way management expects, they may end up in a scenario where debt servicing costs eat into operating income and mitigate their flexibility.

Then there’s the RPO concentration issue, as the $553 billion in RPO is loaded with large-scale AI contracts. ORCL acknowledged that most of the Q3 RPO increase came from these deals, which is a difficult spot to be in, considering AI infrastructure is a fast-moving market and customer preferences can shift overnight. A slowdown in AI model training from efficiency improvements or a shift from training to inference workloads could create problems for Oracle Cloud infrastructure. Recently, OpenAI walked away from 600 megawatts of Abilene expansion because it wanted newer chips. That’s exactly the kind of thing that can happen when you’re building physical infrastructure for a market that moves quickly. ORCL’s whole plan is to grow its way out of this debt, and if RPO conversion slows or contracts get restructured, the math behind ORCL’s profit model gets ugly quickly.

The future buildout has its own set of problems that ORCL doesn’t have control over. We have already seen labor shortages and material constraints cause delays. Power availability is a bottleneck everywhere in the data center industry right now, and if they can’t secure access to continuous power, their data center plans become worthless. GPU generations are turning over faster than buildings can be constructed, so there’s a real risk that by the time a facility is done, the hardware may not meet the customers' demands. If ORCL can’t deliver data centers on time, the carrying costs will negatively impact the top and bottom lines, and that will impact the balance sheet even more.

Conclusion

ORCL’s Q3 was a strong quarter as RPO increased 325%, cloud infrastructure revenue accelerated to 84% YoY, and management guided for $90 billion in revenue for next year. ORCL is putting itself right in the center of the AI infrastructure race, and I think the market is underpricing what that could be worth over the next several years. Based on today’s forward earnings, ORCL looks cheap, but it's priced this way for a very good reason. ORCL has $124.72 billion in debt, $50 billion in new capital raises, and a balance sheet that is stretched unlike its peers. If ORCL can deliver on its projections, its share price could rip higher, but if something goes wrong, such as construction delays or a pullback in AI spending, then we could see ORCL experience a credit downgrade. This is a high-risk, high-reward scenario because there is so much leverage on ORCL’s balance sheet. I’m bullish on ORCL here because I believe the amount they can scale their cash from operations over the next several years will make up for the large-scale buildout that the market is worried about.
